did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (CANFIS) for the detection and classification of melanoma skin cancer images. The study compared the accuracy of CNN (98.31%) against CANFIS (87.61%) using 20 melanoma images from the UCI machine learning repository.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is an original experimental study presented at the 2022 International Conference on Cyber Resilience (ICCR). It reports on the development and comparison of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (CANFIS) models for melanoma detection using a dataset of 20 images from the UCI repository. The paper includes sections for Abstract, Introduction, Materials and Methods, Results (including statistical analysis with p-values), and Conclusion, which are characteristic of primary research. Although the paper was later retracted by IEEE due to quality issues, its original classification and structure are that of a research paper.

title: Detection and classification of Melanoma image of skin cancer based on Convolutional Neural Network and comparison with Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System
brief_summary: This study aimed to improve the accuracy of melanoma skin cancer detection by comparing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) against a Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (CANFIS). The researchers utilized a dataset of 20 melanoma images obtained from the UCI machine learning repository. The CNN model achieved an accuracy of 98.31%, while the CANFIS model achieved 87.61%. Statistical analysis using an independent t-test revealed a significant difference between the two methods with a p-value of 0.001. The results indicated that the CNN classifier provided superior performance compared to the CANFIS algorithm for this specific task. However, the paper was later retracted by IEEE due to incoherent technical language and quality issues.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study exclusively used 20 Melanoma images from the UCI machine learning repository for the classification task. The paper focuses on detecting and classifying this specific malignant skin lesion.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma skin cancer detection (binary: melanoma vs. non-melanoma or healthy skin)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is 'detection and classification of Melanoma image of skin cancer'. It uses a dataset of 20 Melanoma images to train a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and compares it with a Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (CANFIS). The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (presence of melanoma/skin cancer) and report classification metrics such as accuracy (98.31% for CNN, 87.61% for CANFIS), specificity, and sensitivity.

datasets_used.name: UCI Machine Learning Repository
datasets_used.description: A public dataset collection accessed via an open access website, specifically used to obtain images for the study.

use.training: Used as input for the Convolutional Neural Network (Group 1) and Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (Group 2). The paper states '20 Melanoma images (MI) are used for Convolutional Neural Network (Group 1) and it is compared with Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (Group 2)'. It also notes '20 skin disease pictures as input' for each group, implying the same 20 images were used for both models in the comparison.
use.testing: Used for performance evaluation (accuracy, specificity, sensitivity) of the CNN and CANFIS models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity between the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (CANFIS) groups.

statistical_methods_used.method: Levene’s Test for Equality of Variance
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the assumption of homogeneity of variances prior to performing the independent t-test.
To estimate the range of the difference in means between the CNN and CANFIS groups for accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity.
To summarize the central tendency and dispersion of accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity metrics for both groups over 20 iterations.
To determine statistical significance of the differences between the two methods.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the mean accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity between the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (CANFIS) groups.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the mean accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity between the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (CANFIS) groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The p-value was 0.001 (2-tailed), which is less than the alpha threshold of 0.05. Therefore, the null hypothesis was rejected, and the difference between the two methods was considered statistically significant, with CNN showing superior performance.

dataset_details.split: Not explicitly split; 20 images used for both CNN and CANFIS groups

UCI Machine Learning Repository.evaluation_setting: Comparison of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) vs Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (CANFIS) over 20 iterations
UCI Machine Learning Repository.notes: The paper reports that CNN achieved better results compared to CANFIS with a significance value of 0.001 (p < 0.05, 2-tailed). The study was retracted by IEEE due to incoherent technical language and quality issues.

architecture_details.implementation_details: The paper describes the CNN as a 'Pack of-highlights model involving profound learning CNN as a classifier'. It mentions using 'Windows tablet application as a point of interaction to the client' which 'speaks with MATLAB application, a pre-prepared classifier is utilized to characterize skin illness of this picture'. The text also references 'Image processing and Enhancement' and feature extraction techniques like 'ABCD rule, GLCM and HOG' in the context of related work or general methodology, but does not explicitly define the CNN's internal layer structure (e.g., number of layers, kernel sizes, activation functions).

preprocessing_techniques: Noise removal (to free images of noise)
Feature extraction using ABCD rule, GLCM, and HOG
conclusions: The study concluded that the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) classifier achieved superior performance compared to the Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (CANFIS) for the detection and classification of melanoma skin cancer images.
The CNN model achieved a mean accuracy of 98.31%, while the CANFIS model achieved a mean accuracy of 87.61%.

Statistical analysis using an independent t-test demonstrated a significant difference between the two methods, with a p-value of 0.001 (p < 0.05, 2-tailed), indicating that the CNN's superior performance was statistically significant.
The CNN model exhibited better specificity (mean 89.11%) and sensitivity (mean 97.66%) compared to the CANFIS model (specificity mean 87.49%, sensitivity mean 90.03%).

The study acknowledged limitations regarding the small dataset size (only 20 images) and potential issues with class imbalance and overtraining, suggesting that performance could be improved with larger datasets or advanced techniques.
The paper was retracted by IEEE due to incoherent, non-standard, or unclear technical language that impacted the reliability of the results.
important_remarks: The paper was retracted by IEEE due to incoherent, non-standard, or unclear technical language ('tortured phrases') that impacted the reliability of the results.
The study utilized an extremely small dataset of only 20 melanoma images from the UCI machine learning repository, which is insufficient for robust deep learning model training and validation.

The methodology contains significant inconsistencies, such as referring to the comparison group as 'Coactive Neuro Fuzzy Inference System (CANFIS)' in the abstract but 'Artificial Neural Network Group 2' in the Materials and Methods section.

The text includes irrelevant statistical definitions (e.g., skewness, kurtosis, moments) and references to unrelated medical conditions (e.g., EEG signals, prostate cancer, bone metastasis, leukemia) that do not pertain to the melanoma classification task.
There is a lack of clarity regarding the CNN architecture details, with no specific layer structures, kernel sizes, or activation functions provided.
The authors did not respond to inquiries regarding the retraction, raising concerns about research integrity and accountability.
The statistical analysis claims significance (p=0.001) but relies on a sample size of 20 per group without addressing potential overfitting or class imbalance issues inherent in such a small dataset.
